methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $1.0B - $23.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 20.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Cerebras
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $23B

private.name: Credo Semiconductor
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $5B+

private.name: Aeva Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $1B+

data.narrative:
Ouster is a publicly traded semiconductor company founded by Angus Pacala. The peer universe consists of three same-sector, same-stage public comparables: Cerebras ($23.0B), Credo Semiconductor ($5.0B), and Aeva Technologies ($1.0B). The broader comp set includes eight additional private frontier-tech semiconductor firms.

The peer-median method is the applicable triangulation approach here. Across the three public semiconductor peers, the median valuation stands at $5.0B, with a range spanning $1.0B to $23.0B. This method directly anchors to companies operating in identical sector and stage conditions. The public-comp anchoring method—which would apply a 20.3x price-to-sales multiple observed across public semiconductors (ALAB, NVDA, AMD)—cannot be applied due to absence of Ouster's disclosed revenue figures, common among private-stage targets.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $4.2B to $5.8B, centered on a median of $5.0B. This range sits substantially above Ouster's current stated valuation of $1.5B. The primary limitation is methodological: triangulation relies on a single applicable method with only three direct comparables, introducing material sensitivity to peer selection. Key uncertainties include revenue scale relative to peers and whether the wide dispersion among comparables ($1.0B–$23.0B) reflects genuine differentiation or stage-dependent pricing variance. Additional revenue disclosure would enable multiples-based validation.
